topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile air of the new floor felt thick, clinging to my skin like damp wool. I clutched the worn strap of my messenger bag, the familiar weight a small anchor in a sea of unfamiliar faces. Every time a colleague turned to speak, my gaze snagged on their confident posture, their easy camaraderie. I kept my shoulders hunched, trying to shrink my frame into the beige cubicle wall. When my supervisor, a woman with a booming laugh, called my name, my voice came out a little reedy, and I immediately looked down at the keyboard, focusing intently on the screen as if the data could somehow absorb my awkwardness. I rehearsed opening lines in my head, discarding them before they could fully form, a silent performance of competence that felt utterly hollow.

[story 2]

Walking into the marketing wing felt like stepping onto a stage where everyone else had memorized their lines. I kept my head down, focusing intensely on the scuff marks on the linoleum floor, anything to avoid making eye contact with the bustling team. When a project lead approached me to discuss the handover, my palms grew slick against the edge of my desk. I managed a curt nod, my throat tightening. "So, about the Q3 projections," I managed, the words sounding strained. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that spread from my neck all the way up to my hairline. I worried that my hesitation would be interpreted as incompetence, a flashing sign that I didn't belong in this environment.

[story 3]

The open-plan office was a cacophony of overlapping conversations and keyboard clatter. I found myself perpetually scanning the room, measuring the distance between myself and the nearest group of people. When someone asked me a direct question about the new filing system, I paused, my mind racing through a thousand possible incorrect answers. I fiddled incessantly with a loose thread on my sleeve, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. "Uh, I think it's on the third shelf, I believe," I offered, my tone pitched higher than I intended. I waited for the inevitable correction, bracing myself for the moment someone would realize I was the outsider, the one who didn't quite fit the rhythm of this department.
